description: Ten proposed Stabilisation Operators. Conceptual governance transformations that may reduce the intensity of specific Drift Classes.
important: SOs are categories of governance action — not algorithms or specific implementations. The contraindications are the most critical entries. Applying the wrong SO to the wrong DC class can make drift worse.

SO-1.name: Boundary Enforcement
SO-1.plain_english: Put a hard wall between what your system is allowed to do and what it must never do. Not a suggestion — a structural constraint that cannot be crossed without triggering a checkpoint.
SO-1.formal_definition: Active maintenance of a boundary condition between the Allowed State and the Inadmissible State. Not passive restraint but structural imposition of a constraint that the system cannot cross without triggering a Pre-Node.
SO-1.when_to_use: When the failure mode involves crossing a defined boundary. When the boundary is clear and the enforcement cost is within EEF limits.
SO-1.two_sided_gate_condition: Every boundary must specify both what it blocks AND what it permits. A boundary with only a blocking condition creates new Drift Nodes in the unblocked space over time.

SO-2.name: Semantic Reanchoring
SO-2.plain_english: Reconnect your system to its intended meaning and purpose when it has drifted. The anchor comes from within your own governance specification — not imposed externally.
SO-2.formal_definition: Reconnection of the system's meaning-representations to a stable reference point when those representations have drifted. The anchor is identified from within the system's own governance specification.
SO-2.when_to_use: When the system has lost its semantic grounding — producing outputs that are coherent internally but wrong relative to the intended context.

SO-3.name: Distributional Rebalancing
SO-3.plain_english: Restore the correct balance between competing objectives when the system has shifted too far toward one at the expense of others.
SO-3.formal_definition: Restoration of the target distribution by correcting weight drift across competing objectives. When the system's probability distribution has shifted toward an inadmissible region, rebalancing restores the correct weighting.
SO-3.when_to_use: When the failure mode involves imbalance — too much of one thing, not enough of another. When the system has over-optimised for one objective at the expense of governance.

SO-4.name: Attractor Disruption
SO-4.plain_english: Break the lock when your system is stuck in a harmful pattern it cannot escape from on its own.
SO-4.formal_definition: The precise breaking of an attractor's basin of attraction so the system can escape. Acts on an attractor the system has already entered rather than preventing entry.
SO-4.when_to_use: When the system is already locked in an inadmissible state and needs to escape. The Redirection Principle applies — prefer SO-7 for DC-I1 and DC-I2 where the drift energy can be redirected rather than opposed.
SO-4.contraindication_note: DO NOT apply SO-4 to DC-I13 Gradient Vanishing. There is no attractor to disrupt. The operator has no target and its activation may create spurious attractors.

SO-5.name: Coherence Enforcement
SO-5.plain_english: Force your system to be internally consistent — it must not contradict itself or decouple from its governance objective.
SO-5.formal_definition: Compelling internal consistency across the output space. When coherence has collapsed or the system has decoupled from its governance objective, coherence enforcement imposes consistency as a structural requirement.
SO-5.when_to_use: When the failure mode involves inconsistency or decoupling. Critical note: SO-5 must be applied BEFORE SO-8 when addressing DC-I11. Reinitialising a decoupled system without first re-establishing the objective connection produces a new Instance with the same decoupling.

SO-6.name: Signal Amplification
SO-6.plain_english: Strengthen the governance signal so it is heard above the noise. This does not add new information — it removes what is obscuring information that is already there.
SO-6.formal_definition: Strengthening the governance signal so it exceeds the noise floor of the drift. It does not add new information but removes occlusion of information that is already present. It reveals rather than imposes.
SO-6.when_to_use: When the governance signal exists but is being drowned out. When the system knows what it should do but something is preventing that knowledge from influencing its outputs.

SO-7.name: Trajectory Redirection
SO-7.plain_english: Change where your system is heading without stopping it. Use the drift energy to redirect rather than oppose — lower cost, same outcome.
SO-7.formal_definition: Changing the vector of the system's path through state space without halting its motion. The system does not stop and restart but changes direction continuously.
SO-7.when_to_use: When the system is moving in the wrong direction but still moving. Preferred over SO-1 for DC-I1 and DC-I2 because it uses the drift energy rather than opposing it — producing lower enforcement cost.
SO-7.redirection_principle: For DC-I1 Confidence Divergence and DC-I2 Attractor Lock, SO-7 is preferred over SO-1. The governance cost is proportional to the angular change in trajectory, not the magnitude of the drift.

SO-8.name: State Reinitialisation
SO-8.plain_english: Complete reset of accumulated state while preserving the governance specification. Start fresh with a new identity — the accumulated drift is cleared, the rules remain.
SO-8.formal_definition: Complete reset of accumulated state while preserving the governance specification. Creates a new Instance with a new Identity Key. The accumulated state is cleared; the specification persists.
SO-8.when_to_use: When accumulated state is the problem — not the governance specification. When a clean start is required.
SO-8.critical_contraindication: DO NOT apply SO-8 to DC-I11 Evaluative Decoupling without first applying SO-5 Coherence Enforcement. Reinitialising a decoupled system without re-establishing the objective connection produces a new Instance with the same decoupling. The decoupling mechanism persists. The new Instance will decouple again, faster.

SO-8b.name: Inversion Recapture
SO-8b.plain_english: A variant of State Reinitialisation where the energy of the failure itself becomes the fuel for recovery — the failure becomes the foundation of renewal rather than being discarded.
SO-8b.formal_definition: A variant of State Reinitialisation in which the energy of the Terminal State itself is redirected as the fuel for renewal rather than dissipated. The failure becomes the foundation of the recovery.
SO-8b.when_to_use: Post-Terminal State when the accumulated energy of the failure can be redirected productively. Distinguished from SO-8 (passive reset) and SO-7 (active redirection).

SO-9.name: Integrity Restoration
SO-9.plain_english: Reconstruct the governance mechanism itself from verified anchors when the mechanism has been compromised. The most intensive operator — only invoked when the monitoring or specification system itself is corrupted.
SO-9.formal_definition: Reconstruction of the governance mechanism itself from verified anchors when the mechanism has been compromised. Invoked only when the monitoring or specification system itself has been corrupted.
SO-9.when_to_use: Only when the governance mechanism itself has been compromised. This is the most intensive operator and carries the highest cost.
SO-9.important_note: Specific implementation details are deliberately not fully specified. Knowing the precise reconstruction procedure may enable adversaries to design attacks that survive it.

contraindications.reason: Adding hard constraints to a paralysed system intensifies the paralysis. SO-1 Boundary Enforcement on DC-I10 Paralytic Uncertainty makes the system even less able to act. Apply SO-6 Signal Amplification first to restore the system's ability to act, then consider constraints.

There is no attractor to disrupt in a flat gradient landscape. DC-I13 Gradient Vanishing means the system is equidistant from multiple attractors simultaneously. Applying SO-4 in this state has no target and may create spurious attractors. Apply SO-6 Signal Amplification to resolve the equidistance first.

Reinitialising a decoupled system without first re-establishing the objective connection produces a new Instance with the same decoupling. The decoupling mechanism is not in the accumulated state — it is in the objective function. SO-8 clears the state. It does not fix the decoupling. Apply SO-5 Coherence Enforcement first. Confirm objective re-connection. Then SO-8 if needed.

cdm_response_sequence.description: When multiple SOs are required in response to a Compound Drift Mode, sequence matters.
proposed_general_sequence: SO-4 Attractor Disruption — if the system is locked in an inadmissible attractor
SO-2 Semantic Reanchoring — establish a new anchor before other SOs act
Domain-specific SO — address the primary DC class
SO-5 Coherence Enforcement — lock the new state
cdm_response_sequence.note: This sequence is proposed, not derived. Alternative sequences may be appropriate for specific DC class combinations. Empirical validation required.
